title: Building History: Students Recreate ENIAC
summary:
In a classroom in Gilbert, Arizona, a group of middle and high school students are building a piece of history. They're constructing a full-scale replica of ENIAC—the Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer, one of the world's first programmable electronic computers. Built in the 1940s, ENIAC was a giant: it filled a room, used thousands of vacuum tubes, and was about a thousand times faster than any other machine at the time.

The project is the brainchild of Tom Burick, a technology instructor at PS Academy, a school for students with autism and other specialized learning needs. Burick is no stranger to building things. Before teaching, he owned a robotics company called White Box Robotics, which sold about 200 robots in 17 countries. But after the 2008 financial crisis forced him to close the business, he turned to teaching.

"I had so many amazing people help me when I was young," Burick says. "They really gave me their time and resources, and changed the trajectory of my life. I thought I need to pay that forward."

Burick himself is neurodivergent—he has dyscalculia, a learning difference that makes traditional math challenging. But he reframes it as a strength. "I have this CAD program that runs in my head 24 hours a day," he says. "I think the reason I was successful in robotics was because of the dyscalculia. To me, it has always been a superpower."

His students, many of whom are also neurodivergent, bring their own strengths to the project: hyperfocus, precision, and the ability to repeat tasks meticulously. "I didn't want my technology program to feel like craft hour," Burick says. The ENIAC replica, built for the 80th anniversary of the original, is a testament to what students can achieve when they leverage their unique abilities.

The replica precisely matches what ENIAC looked like before it was disassembled in the 1950s. It's a hands-on history lesson that connects students to the roots of modern computing. Burick hopes it inspires them to see their own differences as superpowers, too.
why_it_matters: This project shows how neurodivergent students can excel when given tasks that match their unique abilities, challenging stereotypes about learning differences and demonstrating that inclusive education can produce remarkable achievements in history, technology, and engineering.

keywords.explanation: Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer, one of the first programmable electronic computers, built in the 1940s.
Old-fashioned electronic components that control electric current; early computers used thousands of them.
An exact copy of an object, built to look like the original.
A developmental condition that affects how a person communicates and interacts with others, and often brings focused interests.
A learning difference that makes it hard to understand numbers and math, similar to dyslexia but with numbers.
A term for people whose brain works differently from what is considered typical, including autism, ADHD, dyslexia, and dyscalculia.

questions.question: Who is leading the project to build the ENIAC replica?
What school is hosting the project?
What was Tom Burick's job before teaching?

options: for the 80th anniversary of the original ENIAC
as a class project on history
questions.correct_answer: for the 80th anniversary of the original ENIAC

background_read: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was built in the 1940s at the University of Pennsylvania. It used 18,000 vacuum tubes, weighed 30 tons, and could perform 5,000 additions every second – a thousand times faster than any existing mechanical calculator.

Neurodivergence refers to conditions like autism, ADHD, dyslexia, and dyscalculia, where the brain processes information differently than the majority of people. Many neurodivergent individuals have unique strengths, such as intense focus (hyperfocus), pattern recognition, and meticulous attention to detail.

Tom Burick is a former robotics entrepreneur who founded White Box Robotics. After the 2008 financial crisis closed his company, he turned to teaching at PS Academy, a school that specializes in supporting students with autism and other specialized learning needs.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Students at a special school are building a full-scale replica of ENIAC
├─ Former robotics CEO (White Box Robotics, sold ~200 robots in 17 countries)
└─ Has dyscalculia, reframes it as a superpower
└─ Use hyperfocus, precision, meticulous repetition as strengths
PROJECT: Replica matches ENIAC before it was disassembled in the 1950s
└─ Built for the 80th anniversary of the original
GOAL: Hands-on history lesson connecting students to computing roots
THEME: Neurodiversity as a strength – Burick wants students to see their differences as superpowers

perspectives.description: Students and educators see the replica as empowering; it builds confidence, provides real engineering experience, and celebrates neurodiversity as a source of innovation.
Some may argue the project is too expensive or niche, diverting resources from core academic subjects, or that its success depends heavily on the unique skill of one teacher.

The replica is both a teaching tool and a historical preservation effort. It highlights how specialized education programs can tailor learning to individual strengths, but it remains a case study rather than a scalable model.

If successful, the project could inspire other schools to create hands-on history or engineering projects for neurodivergent students, and may shift public perception of learning differences from deficits to assets.
